column62: A large number of the Kjempevise-melodier or ancient heroic ballads have been preserved in the Province of Telemark. The most famous of them all is the so-called Draumkvedet -- the Vision of Heaven and Hell --which dates back to the 12th Century. It describes the legendary dream of a man who slept from Christmas Eve to Epiphany and the visions he wit-nessed of souls in purgatory. Most of these old ballads are in the nature of dance songs in which the leader of the group renders the narrative while others join in the refrain. //SMITHSONIAN FOLKWAYS, Songs and Dances of Norway, CATALOG NUMBER FW04008_207//

column61: “While visiting a friendly king, King Solomon is asked about his family. Ashamed of having only three daughters, he resorts to a lie, pretending to have three sons. His host now asks the Jewish king to send him one of them. Much distressed. Solomon returns home, deciding to send him one of his daughters disguised as a man. In order to test his daughters courage, he dresses up as a robber and waits in ambush for each of them. The youngest passes the test well and is thus chosen to travel to the foreign court.” //AWM CD 12367 IN LOEB; INFO IN LINER NOTES//

column61: Over the centuries, the Khmer people have believed in natural spirits, souls (pralung), territorial spirits (neak ta), and all that preserves and protects the earth. Traditionally, these spirits and forces have been respected and feared. Arek has been traditionally performed in order to serve the needs of these spiritual forces and show them continual gratitude. When someone is sick, Arek music is played to call the spirit to come, entering a person who is then asked the reasons for the illness. Once the sick person is healthy again, the Laing roang ceremony is performed in which all the protective spirits are invited for feast and to listen to festive music. Offerings of food and drink, along with Arek music, are offered to the spirits in times when there is no rain, if animals or humans in the region are struck by disease, or when a large and important undertaking is planned. These ceremonies ask the spirits for help to solve a problem or to fulfill a wish or desired plan.

column61: Kunwinjku land lies in the area famous for traditional Aboriginal rock art. Artists belong to clans and visually represent the ancestral creation stories or ‘Dreamings’ of their clan. Each clan has an estate where they hunt and gather edible plants and other necessities. Ancestral beings are believed to have created the features of the land. Spirits that animate an individual’s life reside there and at their death the spirit goes back to a Dreaming place.//CODING ID 1225 IN GLOBAL JUKEBOX//

column62: Remember the arrows he shot from his bow, Remember your chiefs by his hatchet laid low, Why so slow? Do you wait 'till I shrink from the pain? No, the son of Alknomook will never complain. Remember the wood where in ambush we lay, and the scalps which we bore from your nation away, Now the flame rises saft, you exult my pain, But the son of Alknomook can never complain. I go to the land where my father is gone, His ghost shall rejoyce in the fame of his son, Death comes like a friend, he relieves me from pain, And thy son, O Alknomook, has scorn'd to complain //FLUTOPEDIA, RELEVANT INFORMATION FROM THE LINK//
